stem: A term male infant born at 39+4 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth is reviewed on the postnatal ward at 72 hours of age. Birthweight was 3460 g and current weight is 3260 g (5.8 percent loss). He is exclusively breastfed and feeding 3 hourly with audible swallowing, has had 4 wet nappies in the last 24 hours and passed meconium and changing stools. Mother is blood group O positive with negative antibody screen; the cord direct antiglobulin test was negative and cord bilirubin was 38 micromol/L. Maternal serology was unremarkable and there is no family history of haemolytic disease, G6PD deficiency or significant neonatal jaundice. The midwife notes that the baby looks visibly yellow to the mid-thigh and orders a serum bilirubin which returns at 340 micromol/L total, with a conjugated fraction of 8 micromol/L. The baby is alert, has a normal Moro and suck, no hepatosplenomegaly, and is afebrile.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 138 bpm, RR 44 /min, SpO2 99 percent on room air
data_table.Examination: Visibly jaundiced to mid-thigh, alert, normal tone and Moro, no hepatosplenomegaly, anterior fontanelle soft, no petechiae or bruising
data_table.Investigations: Total serum bilirubin 340 micromol/L, conjugated 8 micromol/L, Hb 175 g/L, reticulocytes 3 percent, blood group A positive, DAT negative
data_table.Maternal history: Group O positive, antibody screen negative, no maternal infection, no medication exposure

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure, continue breastfeeding on demand and repeat serum bilirubin within 24 hours
options.rationale: SBR 340 micromol/L at 72 h in a term baby crosses the AU phototherapy threshold on the postnatal nomogram; conservative monitoring risks rising levels. Would never be correct above threshold (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice).

options.text: Stop breastfeeding for 24 hours and substitute formula to exclude breast milk jaundice before treating
options.rationale: Routine cessation of breastfeeding is not recommended; continuing feeds with phototherapy is preferred. Would never be correct as routine (Source: RCH CPG; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Commence intensive phototherapy and repeat SBR in 6 hours, exchange if rising towards 450 micromol/L
options.rationale: At 72 hours of age in a term infant with no risk factors, 340 micromol/L exceeds the Australian phototherapy line on the consensus nomogram and intensive phototherapy is started, with exchange transfusion considered if SBR approaches roughly 450 micromol/L despite intensive phototherapy, per RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice.

options.text: Arrange immediate double-volume neonatal exchange transfusion in the neonatal unit today
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion is for SBR approaching 450 micromol/L despite intensive phototherapy or for acute bilirubin encephalopathy. Would be correct at SBR approaching 450 or with neuro signs (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Order abdominal ultrasound and split bilirubin trend to investigate biliary atresia first
options.rationale: Conjugated fraction 8 is normal (under 20 percent of total or under 25 micromol/L) and does not indicate cholestatic workup. Would be correct only with elevated conjugated fraction (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Phototherapy thresholds in Australia are read off a postnatal age vs SBR nomogram with separate lines for risk factors (prematurity, haemolysis, sepsis). A term low-risk infant with 340 micromol/L at 72 hours sits above the phototherapy line; exchange transfusion is reserved for levels nearing approximately 450 micromol/L despite intensive phototherapy or for clinical encephalopathy.
key_points: Threshold depends on postnatal age in hours and risk factors, not just absolute level
Conjugated bilirubin > 25 micromol/L or > 20 percent of total prompts cholestatic workup
Intensive phototherapy means high-irradiance light over maximal skin area
Re-check SBR 4 to 6 hours after starting phototherapy
explanation.pearls: Visible jaundice past the umbilicus in a term newborn always warrants a measured bilirubin; clinical estimation alone is unreliable.

stem: A male infant born at 36+2 weeks by spontaneous vaginal birth to a mother with gestational diabetes on diet control is reviewed on the postnatal ward in a level 4 maternity unit. Birthweight is 2640 g (25th centile). He had skin-to-skin contact from delivery and a first breastfeed at 40 minutes of life. The local hypoglycaemia protocol mandates 3 hourly prefeed BSLs for the first 12 hours in at-risk infants. At 2 hours of age the prefeed heelprick capillary blood glucose, confirmed on a calibrated point-of-care device, is 1.8 mmol/L. He latches briefly but tires quickly and falls asleep at the breast within 2 minutes. He is pink, has a strong cry on handling, normal tone, and a normal newborn examination with no dysmorphic features, no hepatomegaly, no midline defects, and no respiratory distress. He has had no jitteriness, no apnoea, and no seizures. He has not yet received expressed colostrum or formula top-up. The mother's last HbA1c was 5.6 percent and she had no insulin during labour. Maternal observations are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C axillary, HR 142 bpm, RR 48 /min, SpO2 98 percent on room air
data_table.Examination: Alert, pink, normal tone, no jitteriness, no respiratory distress, no hepatomegaly, anterior fontanelle soft
data_table.Investigations: Capillary BSL 1.8 mmol/L at 2 hours of age, prefeed
data_table.Risk factors: Late preterm 36+2, infant of diet-controlled GDM mother, birthweight 25th centile, breastfeeding established but ineffective

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Continue breastfeeding on demand and recheck BSL in 3 to 4 hours before the next feed
options.rationale: BSL 1.8 is below operational threshold 2.6 for at-risk infants and requires active intervention. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Hypoglycaemia).

options.text: IV 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg bolus, 10% dextrose infusion at 80 mL/kg/day, continue feeds, recheck BSL 30 min
options.rationale: A BSL below 2.0 mmol/L in an at-risk infant warrants immediate IV 10 percent dextrose 2 mL/kg bolus and maintenance infusion at around 80 mL/kg/day (delivering approximately 5.5 mg/kg/min of glucose), with rapid recheck, per RCH CPG: Neonatal hypoglycaemia.

options.text: Offer 5 mL/kg of expressed breast milk or formula by cup and recheck BSL in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Enteral feeding is for BSL 2.0-2.6 in asymptomatic at-risk infants; below 2.0 requires IV dextrose. Would be correct at BSL 2.0-2.6 (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Administer IM glucagon 30 microg/kg as a single bolus dose and continue exclusive breastfeeding without recheck
options.rationale: Glucagon is for refractory hypoglycaemia where IV access delayed/impossible and requires hepatic glycogen stores; not first-line in late-preterm IDM with IV access. Would be correct only with delayed IV access (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Apply 40 percent buccal dextrose gel 0.5 mL/kg and continue exclusive breastfeeding without recheck
options.rationale: Buccal dextrose gel plus feeding is for BSL 2.0-2.6 in asymptomatic infants, not below 2.0; recheck always required. Would be correct at BSL 2.0-2.6 (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Operational thresholds in Australian neonatal units are 2.6 mmol/L for at-risk asymptomatic infants and 2.0 mmol/L as the level requiring IV dextrose. Symptomatic hypoglycaemia or BSL under 2.0 mmol/L gets a 10 percent dextrose 2 mL/kg bolus plus infusion at 60 to 90 mL/kg/day, titrated to keep BSL above 2.6 mmol/L.
key_points: At-risk groups: late preterm, IDM, SGA, LGA, perinatal stress
BSL 2.0 to 2.6 mmol/L asymptomatic: feed plus or minus buccal dextrose gel and recheck
BSL under 2.0 mmol/L or any symptomatic infant: IV 10 percent dextrose 2 mL/kg then infusion
Always recheck within 30 minutes of any intervention
explanation.pearls: Glucose delivery in mg/kg/min equals (percent dextrose x mL/kg/day) divided by 144; an 80 mL/kg/day infusion of 10 percent dextrose delivers about 5.5 mg/kg/min.

stem: A 38-year-old G3P2 woman at 41+3 weeks is in second-stage labour when the obstetric team note thick meconium-stained liquor after artificial rupture of membranes. She had two previous uncomplicated vaginal births at term and her current antenatal course was unremarkable with normal serial growth scans and normal screening serology. CTG is reassuring with normal baseline variability and no late or variable decelerations. The baby is born vaginally 12 minutes later with a single nuchal cord that was easily reduced. At delivery the baby cries vigorously within the first 15 seconds, has good tone with flexed limbs, is pink centrally with peripheral cyanosis, and has a heart rate above 100 bpm by auscultation. There is visible thick meconium on the perineum, on the baby's skin and around the mouth. The neonatal team is present at delivery with a prepared resuscitaire, the radiant warmer is on, and suction is checked. No previous obstetric complications.

data_table.Birth details: Term plus, 41+3 weeks, vaginal birth, thick meconium-stained liquor
data_table.Examination at 30 seconds: Vigorous cry, good tone, HR > 100 bpm, peripheral cyanosis only, visible meconium on skin

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Routine newborn care: drying, warmth, ongoing observation, keep mother and baby together
options.rationale: A vigorous newborn with meconium-stained liquor does not require tracheal suctioning; routine care with drying, warming and observation is recommended, per ANZCOR Guideline 13.4 and the Australian NRP.

options.text: Direct laryngoscopy with tracheal suction before drying or stimulation today
options.rationale: Routine tracheal suction of vigorous meconium-stained newborns is no longer recommended and may delay resuscitation. Would be correct only in non-vigorous newborn with airway obstruction (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).

options.text: Bag-mask ventilation with 21 percent oxygen for 60 seconds and reassess heart rate
options.rationale: PPV is indicated only with apnoea, gasping, or HR under 100, none of which apply. Would be correct in those situations (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).

options.text: Intubate, suction the trachea and transfer to NICU for prophylactic CPAP
options.rationale: Intubation is for ineffective bag-mask or specific indications; prophylactic CPAP/intubation not indicated in vigorous term newborn. Would be correct only with failed PPV (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).

options.text: Bulb suction the oropharynx and nasopharynx on the perineum before delivery of the shoulders
options.rationale: Intrapartum oro/nasopharyngeal suctioning on the perineum is no longer recommended and does not reduce MAS. Would never be correct (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).

explanation.summary: Since the 2015 ILCOR update, ANZCOR has recommended that vigorous newborns with meconium-stained liquor receive routine care without tracheal suctioning. Non-vigorous infants (poor tone, apnoea, HR less than 100 bpm) go to the resuscitaire and follow the standard algorithm; tracheal suction is only considered if ventilation is ineffective and meconium is suspected to obstruct the airway.
key_points: Vigorous = good tone, good cry or breathing, HR > 100 bpm
Routine intrapartum oro/nasopharyngeal suction on the perineum is not recommended
Non-vigorous meconium-stained newborn: dry, stimulate, then standard NRP
Tracheal suction only if PPV is ineffective and meconium obstruction is suspected
explanation.pearls: The change reduces delay to effective ventilation, which is the single most important resuscitation step.

stem: A male infant born at 28+1 weeks by emergency caesarean for severe pre-eclampsia and absent end-diastolic flow on Doppler is now day 7 of life in the level 3 NICU of a tertiary perinatal centre. Mother received a full course of antenatal betamethasone and magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection. Birthweight was 1040 g (25th centile for gestation). He has been receiving minimal enteral feeds of expressed breast milk at 20 mL/kg/day for 4 days, advanced cautiously, and parenteral nutrition via a peripherally inserted central catheter; umbilical lines were removed on day 4. He is on nasal CPAP with FiO2 0.21 after extubation from surfactant therapy on day 2. Over the last 6 hours the nurses note new abdominal distension, two episodes of bilious aspirate, a single small bloody stool and a temperature instability with a low of 36.1 deg C. On examination he is mottled with a capillary refill of 4 seconds, the abdomen is tense and tender with visible bowel loops, and there is mild erythema of the abdominal wall. The treating team obtains a supine abdominal radiograph and a left lateral decubitus film, plus blood cultures, a venous gas and a full blood count.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.1 deg C, HR 178 bpm, RR 70 /min on CPAP FiO2 0.21, SpO2 91 percent
data_table.Examination: Mottled, tense distended tender abdomen, visible bowel loops, abdominal wall erythema, lethargic
data_table.Investigations: WCC 4.2 x10^9/L, platelets 78 x10^9/L, CRP 65 mg/L, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, glucose 3.1 mmol/L
data_table.Imaging: Supine AXR shows dilated bowel loops with intramural curvilinear lucencies; left lateral decubitus film shows no free intraperitoneal gas; no portal venous gas seen

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue current feeds, add oral probiotic, and repeat AXR in 24 hours
options.rationale: Continuing feeds in confirmed NEC (Bell IIA with pneumatosis) is contraindicated and risks progression to perforation. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: NEC).

options.text: Increase enteral feed volume to advance gut maturation and start oral gentamicin for bowel decontamination
options.rationale: Advancing feeds is contraindicated; oral aminoglycosides not indicated for established NEC; bowel rest with parenteral broad-spectrum is standard. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Urgent laparotomy with bowel resection for suspected NEC-related intestinal perforation today
options.rationale: Surgery is for pneumoperitoneum, fixed bowel loop, palpable mass or deterioration despite medical therapy; Bell IIA without perforation is medical with surgical review on standby. Would be correct only with perforation or deterioration (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Cease feeds, large-bore OG free drainage, IV benzylpenicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole, fluid resuscitation, surgical review
options.rationale: Bell stage IIA NEC (definite, with pneumatosis intestinalis) is managed medically with bowel rest, orogastric decompression, parenteral broad-spectrum antibiotics covering Gram-negative and anaerobic organisms (benzylpenicillin or ampicillin plus gentamicin plus metronidazole) and supportive care, per RCH CPG: Necrotising enterocolitis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Cease feeds, give a single dose of IV cefotaxime and continue monitoring without surgical review
options.rationale: Cefotaxime monotherapy does not adequately cover anaerobes; surgical review is mandatory at diagnosis. Would never be correct as sole therapy (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: NEC peaks in preterm infants in the second week of life. Bell staging: I suspected; IIA definite with pneumatosis intestinalis; IIB with portal venous gas or ascites; IIIA severe medical disease; IIIB perforation. IIA management is bowel rest, OG decompression, triple antibiotics (benzylpenicillin or ampicillin, gentamicin, metronidazole) for 7 to 10 days, parenteral nutrition, and serial AXR every 6 to 12 hours.
key_points: Pneumatosis intestinalis is pathognomonic for NEC
Portal venous gas suggests advanced disease (Bell IIB)
Free intraperitoneal air on lateral decubitus or cross-table lateral indicates perforation and surgery
Triple antibiotic cover for Gram-negative and anaerobic organisms
explanation.pearls: Falling platelet count and rising lactate are markers of deterioration and prompt surgical review even without radiographic perforation.

stem: A male infant born at 39+0 weeks by elective caesarean section without labour is admitted to the special care nursery at 1 hour of life for ongoing respiratory distress. Birthweight 3520 g, APGARs 8 and 9. He developed tachypnoea, mild grunting and intercostal recession from 15 minutes of age. He is now 4 hours old on nasal high-flow at 6 L/min with FiO2 0.30, maintaining SpO2 96 percent preductal and 96 percent postductal. Mother had a negative group B Strep screen, no fever, no prolonged rupture of membranes, no diabetes and no anaesthetic complications; no meconium-stained liquor. T 36.9 deg C, HR 152, RR 78, BP 62/40 with capillary refill 2 seconds and warm peripheries. Chest is clear with no asymmetry. Chest radiograph at 30 minutes showed hyperinflation, prominent perihilar streaking, fluid in the right horizontal fissure, and no air bronchograms or focal consolidation. He has not yet fed. Blood gas: pH 7.32, pCO2 48 mmHg, BE -2. Sepsis bloods are pending. You are confident this is transient tachypnoea of the newborn (TTN).

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, HR 152 bpm, RR 78 /min, SpO2 97 percent on high-flow FiO2 0.25
data_table.Examination: Mild grunting, intercostal recession, equal air entry bilaterally, no asymmetry, no scaphoid abdomen, normal peripheral pulses
data_table.Imaging: CXR: hyperinflation, prominent perihilar vascular streaking, fluid in the right horizontal fissure, no air bronchograms, no focal consolidation, no pneumothorax

lead_in: Which next management step is most appropriate?

options.text: Continue nasal high-flow titrated to SpO2 91-95 percent, nil by mouth on IV maintenance, reassess at 72 hours
options.rationale: TTN is managed supportively with non-invasive respiratory support (nasal high-flow or CPAP) titrated to keep SpO2 91 to 95 percent, gut rest until work of breathing settles, and IV maintenance fluids while waiting for fetal lung fluid to clear (usually within 24 to 72 hours). Persisting tachypnoea beyond 72 hours mandates reconsideration of the diagnosis (sepsis, cardiac, surfactant deficiency), per RCH CPG: Neonatal respiratory distress and Safer Care Victoria neonatal eHandbook.

options.text: Administer endotracheal surfactant via the INSURE technique now, given the persisting FiO2 requirement at 4 hours of postnatal life
options.rationale: Surfactant therapy is for respiratory distress syndrome (preterm, ground-glass with air bronchograms, surfactant deficiency); the CXR pattern and term gestation here are inconsistent with RDS, and surfactant is not indicated for TTN, per RCH CPG: Neonatal respiratory distress.

options.text: Commence empirical IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin pending blood culture results and continue the current respiratory support level
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics in a term infant with negative maternal GBS, no fever, no prolonged ROM and a CXR pattern characteristic of TTN are not routinely required; antibiotics are reserved for clinical concern for sepsis or persisting illness beyond 72 hours, per RCH CPG: Early onset sepsis.

options.text: Wean nasal high-flow off now and commence demand breastfeeding to encourage fetal lung-fluid clearance and feeding tolerance
options.rationale: Weaning support and commencing feeds while the infant remains in significant respiratory distress (RR 78, grunting, FiO2 0.30) risks aspiration and worsens work of breathing; nil by mouth and continued support are appropriate until respiratory effort settles, per RCH CPG: Neonatal respiratory distress.

options.text: Urgently transfer to a tertiary neonatal intensive care unit for elective intubation and mechanical ventilation now
options.rationale: The infant is stable on non-invasive support with adequate saturations and a normal blood gas; intubation and tertiary transfer are reserved for failing non-invasive support, persistent hypoxaemia, or alternative diagnoses, per RCH CPG: Neonatal respiratory distress.

explanation.summary: TTN results from delayed clearance of foetal lung fluid, classically after elective caesarean without labour. It resolves within 24 to 72 hours with supportive oxygen and CPAP if needed. Key CXR differentiators: RDS = ground-glass with air bronchograms (preterm); MAS = patchy coarse infiltrates with hyperinflation (term with meconium); pneumonia = focal consolidation; pneumothorax = hyperlucent hemithorax.
key_points: TTN: term, caesarean without labour, perihilar streaks and fissural fluid
RDS: preterm, diffuse ground-glass, surfactant deficiency
MAS: term post-dates, meconium liquor, patchy infiltrates
Pneumothorax: asymmetric breath sounds, hyperlucent on transillumination and CXR
explanation.pearls: If tachypnoea persists beyond 72 hours, reconsider the diagnosis.

stem: A term female infant born at 39+5 weeks by spontaneous vaginal birth is reviewed at 12 hours of age on the postnatal ward. Mother is a 28-year-old G2P1 who had a positive GBS rectovaginal swab at 36 weeks of gestation and was penicillin allergic with a remote history of a non-anaphylactic rash documented in her records; she received two doses of IV cefazolin intrapartum, the second dose 6 hours before delivery. Membranes were ruptured for 20 hours. Maternal temperature peaked at 38.2 deg C during labour and white cell count was 16 x10^9/L. The baby is now lethargic with poor feeding for the last 3 hours, has had one episode of grunting respiration with transient desaturation, and looks pale and mottled. She was previously well from birth, with APGARs 9 and 9, no resuscitation required, and birthweight 3300 g. The receiving registrar reviews her promptly and recognises the combination of maternal fever, prolonged ROM and clinical deterioration as warranting urgent septic workup and empirical antibiotics.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.2 deg C axillary, HR 178 bpm, RR 70 /min, SpO2 94 percent on room air, BP 58/32 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Lethargic, pale, mild grunting, mottled peripheries, capillary refill 4 seconds, anterior fontanelle soft, no rash
data_table.Investigations: FBC, blood culture and CRP taken; lumbar puncture deferred pending stabilisation

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: IV cefotaxime twelve-hourly as initial empirical monotherapy
options.rationale: Cefotaxime monotherapy is not standard empirical cover for EOS in AU; synergistic penicillin plus aminoglycoside is preferred. Would be correct only with cefotaxime added to a penicillin-aminoglycoside backbone with meningitis (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin 12-hourly plus gentamicin every 24 hours
options.rationale: Early-onset neonatal sepsis (< 72 hours) is most commonly caused by group B Streptococcus and Escherichia coli; the recommended empirical regimen is benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic and RCH CPG: Early onset sepsis.

options.text: IV flucloxacillin 6-hourly plus gentamicin every 24 hours
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin targets Staph aureus and is for late-onset sepsis with line infection, not first-line cover for EOGBS and E. coli. Would be correct for late-onset with line sepsis (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV vancomycin 12-hourly plus cefotaxime 12-hourly
options.rationale: Vanc plus cefotaxime is for late-onset hospital-acquired sepsis with meningitis suspicion or MRSA risk; not standard for early-onset community-presenting. Would be correct in late-onset (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Withhold antibiotics and observe 4-hourly for 12 hours given intrapartum cefazolin
options.rationale: Intrapartum cefazolin does not exclude sepsis with clinical deterioration; empirical antibiotics must be started. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Early-onset neonatal sepsis (less than 72 hours) is dominated by GBS and Gram-negatives, especially E. coli. Australian empirical therapy is benzylpenicillin (or ampicillin) plus gentamicin. Late-onset sepsis (greater than 72 hours, particularly in the NICU) adds coagulase-negative Staphylococci, S. aureus and Gram-negatives, and typically uses flucloxacillin plus gentamicin or vancomycin in MRSA-prevalent units.
key_points: EOGBS empirical: benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin
Late-onset: flucloxacillin plus gentamicin (community) or vancomycin plus gentamicin (NICU)
Add cefotaxime if meningitis is suspected or proven
Lumbar puncture should be done once the infant is stable enough
explanation.pearls: Cefazolin intrapartum prophylaxis is acceptable in non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy; clindamycin is reserved for anaphylactic allergy with proven susceptibility.

stem: A term female infant is born at 38+4 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth with a birthweight of 2360 g (below the 3rd centile), a head circumference of 31.5 cm (below the 3rd centile), and scattered petechiae on the trunk and limbs noted on first examination. APGARs were 8 and 9 and no resuscitation was required. The mother is a 24-year-old preschool teacher who had a flu-like illness with malaise, sore throat and mild posterior cervical lymphadenopathy at around 14 weeks gestation; the GP attributed it to a viral URTI and routine maternal serology was not repeated and TORCH screening was not done. On day 2 of life, the baby has a moderately enlarged liver palpable 3 cm below the costal margin, a spleen tip palpable, mild conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia and thrombocytopenia on FBC. A cranial ultrasound performed by the neonatologist shows scattered periventricular calcifications and mild ventricular dilatation, prompting paediatric infectious diseases review. Initial automated otoacoustic emission screen on day 2 is REFER bilaterally and is repeated next day with the same result. Audiology brainstem testing is pending. The treating team obtains urine and saliva PCR samples within the first week of life to confirm the suspected diagnosis.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 150 bpm, RR 48 /min, SpO2 99 percent on room air
data_table.Examination: SGA, microcephaly, petechiae on trunk, hepatomegaly 3 cm, mild jaundice
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 62 x10^9/L, total bilirubin 110 micromol/L with conjugated fraction 35 micromol/L, ALT 90 U/L, AST 120 U/L
data_table.Imaging: Cranial ultrasound: periventricular calcifications, mild ventricular dilatation
data_table.Microbiology: Saliva and urine CMV PCR collected on day 3 of life

lead_in: Once congenital CMV is confirmed by PCR within 21 days of life, what is the single most appropriate next management step?

options.text: Oral valganciclovir twice daily for 6 months with weekly FBC/LFT and audiology
options.rationale: Symptomatic congenital CMV with end-organ involvement (microcephaly, hepatitis, thrombocytopenia, intracranial calcifications, or sensorineural hearing loss) treated with oral valganciclovir 16 mg/kg BD for 6 months improves hearing and neurodevelopmental outcomes; haematology and LFT monitoring is required because of neutropenia and hepatotoxicity, per RCH CPG: Congenital CMV and AAP Red Book.

options.text: Observe with serial audiology, no antiviral, treat only confirmed progressive hearing loss after 12 months
options.rationale: Symptomatic congenital CMV warrants prompt antiviral; delaying to 12 months misses the window when valganciclovir improves outcomes. Would never be correct in symptomatic disease (Source: RCH CPG: Congenital CMV).

options.text: Commence IV ganciclovir twice daily for 6 weeks followed by no further therapy
options.rationale: Six weeks IV ganciclovir was older regimen, superseded by 6 months oral valganciclovir (non-inferior, better neurodevelopmental/audiological). Would be correct only if oral not tolerated (Source: AAP Red Book).

options.text: CMV hyperimmune globulin infusion and arrange paediatric infectious diseases service review today
options.rationale: CMV hyperimmune globulin is not standard postnatal therapy for symptomatic congenital CMV; oral valganciclovir is recommended. Would never be correct postnatally (Source: AAP Red Book).

options.text: Treat only audiological component with hearing aid fitting after ABR; provide no antiviral therapy
options.rationale: Hearing rehab is part of care but does not replace antiviral, which improves hearing trajectories. Would never be correct alone (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Congenital CMV is the leading non-genetic cause of sensorineural hearing loss in children. Diagnosis requires viral detection (urine or saliva PCR) within the first 21 days of life to distinguish from postnatal acquisition. Symptomatic infants (CNS, hepatic, haematological or SNHL) benefit from 6 months of oral valganciclovir, with regular FBC for neutropenia and LFTs. All affected children need longitudinal audiology because hearing loss can be late-onset or progressive.
key_points: Saliva PCR is the first-line screening test; urine PCR confirms
Diagnostic window is the first 21 days of life
Treat symptomatic disease with valganciclovir 16 mg/kg BD for 6 months
Monitor for neutropenia, anaemia and transaminitis on treatment
explanation.pearls: Asymptomatic congenital CMV with isolated SNHL is an area of active research; many AU centres treat with valganciclovir, but practice varies.

stem: A male infant is born at 38+1 weeks by spontaneous vaginal birth to a 21-year-old G1P0 woman from a remote community who had limited antenatal care, with her first antenatal visit at 34 weeks of gestation. Routine antenatal screening at that visit returned a reactive treponemal EIA, a non-treponemal RPR titre of 1 in 64, and confirmatory TPPA positive. The mother received a single dose of IM benzathine benzylpenicillin 1.8 g (2.4 million units) at 35 weeks of gestation and a planned second dose was missed because she returned to her community without follow-up. There is no documented prior syphilis treatment and contact tracing of her partner is incomplete. Repeat RPR at 38 weeks remained 1 in 64 with no fourfold drop. HIV, hepatitis B and hepatitis C serology were negative. At birth the baby weighs 2680 g (10th centile), has a maculopapular rash on the palms and soles, hepatosplenomegaly with the liver palpable 3 cm and spleen 2 cm below the costal margins, and clear persistent rhinorrhoea (snuffles). He is alert with normal vital signs and no respiratory distress. APGARs were 8 and 9.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, HR 144 bpm, RR 46 /min, SpO2 99 percent on room air
data_table.Examination: SGA, maculopapular rash palms and soles, hepatosplenomegaly, clear nasal discharge (snuffles), no jaundice
data_table.Investigations: Infant RPR titre 1 in 128 (fourfold higher than maternal), FBC platelets 120 x10^9/L, ALT 70 U/L, long bone radiographs show metaphyseal lucencies; LP CSF VDRL pending

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate treatment regimen for this infant?

options.text: Single IM benzathine benzylpenicillin with outpatient RPR follow-up at 3, 6, 12 months
options.rationale: Single-dose IM benzathine is for infants with normal examination/investigations and adequately treated mothers; this infant has proven congenital syphilis. Would be correct only with normal exam/labs and treated mother (Source: Australian STI Guidelines).

options.text: Oral azithromycin daily for 14 days as macrolide cover for spirochaete infection
options.rationale: Azithromycin is not recommended for congenital syphilis (resistance, CNS penetration); parenteral penicillin is only acceptable treatment. Would never be correct (Source: Australian STI Guidelines).

options.text: Defer treatment until FTA-ABS IgM positive and CSF VDRL reported, then treat only if both confirm
options.rationale: Treatment should not be delayed pending all confirmatory tests when clinical/serological/radiological findings establish probable congenital syphilis. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin q12h first 7 days then q8h, total 10 days, monitor reaction
options.rationale: Proven or highly probable congenital syphilis (fourfold higher RPR than maternal, clinical signs, abnormal radiographs or bloods) requires 10 days of IV benzylpenicillin 30 mg/kg per dose, dosed 12-hourly in the first week and 8-hourly thereafter, per RCH CPG: Congenital syphilis and Australian STI Management Guidelines.

options.text: IV ceftriaxone once daily for 10 days as a penicillin-sparing regimen today
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone has limited data in neonatal congenital syphilis and is not first-line; penicillin desensitisation should be performed if true allergy. Would be correct only with documented severe penicillin allergy after desensitisation failure (Source: Australian STI Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Congenital syphilis is increasing in Australia, particularly in remote and Indigenous communities. Treatment of proven or highly probable disease (fourfold RPR titre difference, clinical features, abnormal CSF/long-bone films/LFTs) is IV benzylpenicillin for 10 days. Asymptomatic infants of inadequately treated mothers can sometimes receive a single IM benzathine dose if the full workup is normal.
key_points: Maternal treatment is inadequate if not benzathine penicillin, less than 4 weeks before delivery, or no fourfold drop in RPR
Long bone films show metaphysitis or periostitis; CSF VDRL confirms neurosyphilis
IV benzylpenicillin 30 mg/kg per dose for 10 days
Follow up RPR titres at 3, 6 and 12 months; expect fourfold decline
explanation.pearls: Antenatal screening in Australia now includes third-trimester syphilis serology in high-prevalence regions; a positive maternal test at 34 weeks should never be missed.

stem: A male infant is born at 38+5 weeks by spontaneous vaginal birth to a 32-year-old G3P2 woman on methadone 80 mg daily through pregnancy as part of an established opioid treatment program at a metropolitan tertiary centre. She has been engaged with antenatal care and the perinatal addictions team throughout pregnancy, attending all scheduled appointments and counselling sessions. There is no other substance use on serial urine drug screens, hepatitis B, hepatitis C and HIV serology are negative, and she has tested negative for syphilis. She smokes 5 cigarettes daily and uses no alcohol. Birthweight is 3120 g (50th centile) and APGARs were 8 and 9 with no resuscitation required. He is breastfeeding well with audible swallows. From 36 hours of age the midwives note increasing irritability, a high-pitched cry, frequent yawning, mild tremor with handling, loose stools that have caused excoriation around the buttocks, and a sleep period of less than one hour after a feed. Modified Finnegan Neonatal Abstinence Scoring System scores done 4-hourly have been 9, 11 and 10 over the last 12 hours despite optimised non-pharmacological care including swaddling, low stimulation environment, demand breastfeeding, and skin-to-skin contact.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.1 deg C, HR 168 bpm, RR 60 /min, SpO2 99 percent on room air
data_table.Examination: Tremulous with handling, mottled, hyperreflexic, no seizures, loose yellow stools, excoriation over the chin, breastfeeding established
data_table.Investigations: Capillary BSL 3.4 mmol/L, FBC and CRP normal, maternal toxicology positive for methadone only

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Switch to formula feeding and continue non-pharmacological care alone without any pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: Breastfeeding is encouraged in stable methadone-maintained mothers and reduces NAS severity; switching to formula is not indicated. Would never be correct as routine (Source: RCH CPG: NAS).

options.text: Commence oral phenobarbital as monotherapy for opioid withdrawal syndrome
options.rationale: Phenobarbital is adjunctive for polysubstance exposure or refractory NAS, not first-line monotherapy for opioid NAS where opioids are the agent of choice. Would be correct as adjunct in polysubstance NAS (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Administer naloxone IM to reverse residual maternal opioid effects in the neonate
options.rationale: Naloxone is contraindicated in babies of opioid-dependent mothers (precipitates severe acute withdrawal/seizures). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Discharge home now with community midwife follow-up and a parent-completed NAS scoring sheet
options.rationale: Persistent Finnegan 8+ requires inpatient monitoring and consideration of pharmacotherapy, not community follow-up. Would be correct only with Finnegan under 8 (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Optimise non-pharmacological care and commence oral morphine q4h titrated to Finnegan below 8
options.rationale: Three consecutive Finnegan scores at or above 8 (or two at or above 12) after maximal non-pharmacological care indicates pharmacological treatment; oral morphine is first-line for opioid-related NAS in Australia, typically starting at 0.04 to 0.05 mg/kg per dose 4-hourly, titrated to scores, per RCH CPG: Neonatal abstinence syndrome and Therapeutic Guidelines: Neonatal.

explanation.summary: NAS from in-utero opioid exposure typically presents at 24 to 72 hours for methadone (later for buprenorphine). Non-pharmacological care (rooming-in, breastfeeding if not contraindicated, swaddling, low stimulation) is first. Pharmacological treatment with oral morphine begins when modified Finnegan scores remain at or above 8 for three consecutive measurements or two at or above 12 despite optimisation.
key_points: Methadone NAS peaks at 48 to 72 hours; buprenorphine later
Breastfeeding is encouraged unless co-use of cocaine, methamphetamine or untreated HIV
Morphine titration to Finnegan less than 8; wean by 10 percent every 24 to 48 hours
Phenobarbital is adjunctive for polysubstance or refractory cases
explanation.pearls: Naloxone in a baby of an opioid-dependent mother can precipitate seizures and is contraindicated even in apparent respiratory depression.

stem: A male infant is born at 40+1 weeks by emergency caesarean for placental abruption after a sentinel maternal collapse 25 minutes before delivery; the mother presented to a regional birthing unit with sudden severe abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding and was transferred urgently to theatre when the CTG showed prolonged bradycardia at 60 bpm. Estimated time of insult to delivery is 22 minutes. APGARs were 1 at 1 minute, 3 at 5 minutes and 5 at 10 minutes. Cord arterial gas shows pH 6.92, base deficit 18 mmol/L, and lactate 11 mmol/L. He required positive pressure ventilation from birth, intubation at 4 minutes for ongoing bradycardia, and three doses of intratracheal then intravenous adrenaline 10 microg/kg via emergency umbilical venous catheter before return of spontaneous circulation at 8 minutes. He is now 45 minutes old, intubated on conventional ventilation in the NICU at a tertiary centre with active servo-controlled cooling preparations underway, including a cooling mattress and oesophageal probe. He has reduced spontaneous activity with intermittent generalised tonic posturing of the limbs lasting around 20 seconds, sluggish pupils that are 3 mm and equally reactive, a weak suck and absent gag, and an exaggerated Moro reflex with prolonged opening. His axillary temperature on arrival was 36.4 deg C and the team has held active warming, allowing passive cooling, while preparing servo-controlled cooling. There is no congenital anomaly, no major birth trauma, and no clinical evidence of bleeding. The treating consultant must decide on definitive neuroprotective therapy in the next 15 minutes, well within the 6 hour therapeutic window from birth.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.4 deg C axillary on arrival, HR 130 bpm post-resuscitation, mean BP 38 mmHg on dobutamine 5 microg/kg/min, SpO2 95 percent on FiO2 0.4
data_table.Examination: Reduced activity, intermittent tonic posturing, weak suck, absent gag, exaggerated Moro, hypotonia in limbs, sluggish pupils, anterior fontanelle soft
data_table.Cord gas: Arterial pH 6.92, base deficit 18 mmol/L, lactate 11 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Permit passive cooling to 35.5 deg C until 24 hours of age then rewarm over 12 hours
options.rationale: Passive cooling to 35.5 degC does not provide target therapeutic range; servo-controlled cooling to 33-34 is required. Would never be correct as definitive (Source: ANZNN Cooling Protocol).

options.text: Maintain strict normothermia 36.5-37.5 deg C and commence prophylactic phenobarbitone loading dose
options.rationale: Normothermia is not the goal in confirmed moderate/severe HIE within 6 h; therapeutic hypothermia is evidence-based neuroprotection. Would never be correct (Source: ANZNN).

options.text: Whole-body therapeutic hypothermia 33-34 deg C for 72 hours with aEEG, rewarm 12 hours, phenobarbitone for seizures
options.rationale: Term or near-term infants (at least 35 weeks gestation) within 6 hours of birth with evidence of perinatal hypoxia-ischaemia and moderate to severe encephalopathy (Sarnat 2 or 3) meet criteria for whole-body cooling to 33 to 34 deg C for 72 hours, with rewarming at 0.5 deg C per hour, per ANZNN Cooling Protocol and Cochrane: Cooling for HIE. Phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IV is first-line for clinical seizures, per RCH CPG: Neonatal seizures.

options.text: Initiate selective head cooling with cap to scalp temperature 28 deg C for 48 hours and rapid rewarming
options.rationale: Selective head cooling is alternative but duration is 72 h not 48, and AU/NZ units predominantly use whole-body cooling. Would be correct only with selective head cooling protocol at correct duration (Source: ANZNN).

options.text: Delay cooling for 12 hours pending MRI brain, commence hypothermia only if injury confirmed
options.rationale: Cooling must start within 6 h to be neuroprotective; waiting for MRI misses the window. Would never be correct (Source: Cochrane HIE).

explanation.summary: Therapeutic hypothermia (whole-body or selective head cooling) reduces death and major neurodevelopmental disability in moderate to severe neonatal encephalopathy. Eligibility: at least 35 weeks gestation, age less than 6 hours, evidence of perinatal hypoxia-ischaemia (cord pH 7.0 or below, base deficit 16 or higher, or APGAR 5 or less at 10 minutes, or ongoing resuscitation), and clinical or aEEG evidence of moderate or severe encephalopathy (Sarnat 2 or 3). Target temperature 33 to 34 deg C for 72 hours, rewarm at 0.5 deg C per hour.
key_points: Window: less than 6 hours of birth
Sarnat 1 (mild) does not require cooling; Sarnat 2 to 3 do
Phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IV is first-line for neonatal seizures
Side effects of cooling: sinus bradycardia, thrombocytopenia, subcutaneous fat necrosis
explanation.pearls: Apparent shivering and decreased seizure threshold during rewarming are recognised; rewarm slowly and monitor aEEG closely.

stem: A male infant is born at 40+3 weeks after a labour complicated by thick meconium-stained liquor and a non-reassuring CTG with late decelerations in the second stage. Birthweight is 3680 g. APGARs were 4 at 1 minute and 7 at 5 minutes. He required brief positive pressure ventilation in the delivery room and is now 6 hours old in the level 3 NICU at a tertiary perinatal centre, on conventional ventilation with FiO2 0.85, peak inspiratory pressure 24 cm H2O, PEEP 6 cm H2O, mean airway pressure 14 cm H2O, and an oxygenation index of 22 calculated from an arterial blood gas with PaO2 50 mmHg. Preductal SpO2 measured on the right hand is 92 percent and postductal SpO2 on the right foot is 78 percent, a sustained difference of 14 percent. Chest radiograph shows patchy coarse infiltrates with hyperinflation consistent with meconium aspiration. Urgent echocardiography by the neonatal cardiologist shows right-to-left shunting across the patent ductus arteriosus, tricuspid regurgitation with an estimated right ventricular systolic pressure of 70 mmHg, septal flattening at end-systole, and structurally normal cardiac anatomy with normal pulmonary venous return on detailed views. Surfactant 100 mg/kg has already been given via the endotracheal tube. He has a normal capillary refill of 2 seconds on milrinone 0.5 microg/kg/min and dobutamine 5 microg/kg/min, with a mean arterial pressure of 42 mmHg.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, mean BP 42 mmHg on inotropes, preductal SpO2 92 percent, postductal SpO2 78 percent, T 36.9 deg C
data_table.Ventilation: Conventional ventilation, FiO2 0.85, mean airway pressure 14 cm H2O, oxygenation index 22
data_table.Echocardiography: Right-to-left ductal shunt, RVSP 70 mmHg, septal flattening, structurally normal heart, normal pulmonary veins

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion to maintain ductal patency
options.rationale: PGE1 is for duct-dependent CHD; structurally normal heart with PPHN does not benefit and PGE1 can worsen pulmonary congestion. Would be correct in duct-dependent CHD (Source: RCH CPG: PPHN).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn

options.text: Inhaled nitric oxide at 20 ppm via ventilator and reassess oxygenation index after 30-60 min
options.rationale: PPHN with an oxygenation index above 20 in a term infant after optimisation of ventilation and surfactant warrants inhaled nitric oxide at a starting dose of 20 ppm; if response is inadequate or OI rises despite iNO, ECMO referral should be considered, per RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn and BMJ Best Practice: PPHN.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn

options.text: Switch to high-frequency oscillatory ventilation immediately and avoid pulmonary vasodilators today
options.rationale: HFOV is useful adjunct to recruit atelectatic lung in MAS and may combine with iNO, but does not replace pulmonary vasodilator therapy in established PPHN. Would be correct as adjunct (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn

options.text: Refer immediately for emergency ECMO without commencing iNO or HFOV trial
options.rationale: ECMO is for refractory PPHN with OI persistently over 25-40 despite iNO and HFOV; bypassing iNO at OI 22 is premature. Would be correct at OI over 25-40 despite optimised therapy (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn

options.text: Commence oral sildenafil every 6 hours as primary pulmonary vasodilator therapy
options.rationale: Oral sildenafil is adjunctive or weaning therapy after iNO; not first-line in acute severe PPHN. Would be correct as weaning therapy (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: PPHN presents as hypoxaemia disproportionate to lung disease with a preductal-postductal SpO2 difference of 10 percent or more and echocardiographic right-to-left shunting and elevated pulmonary pressures. Treatment ladder is optimise ventilation and lung recruitment, correct acidosis, ensure adequate systemic BP, then add iNO at 20 ppm. HFOV combines well with iNO. Sildenafil and milrinone are adjuncts. ECMO is reserved for refractory cases with OI persistently above 25 to 40.
key_points: Differential cyanosis (preductal greater than postductal SpO2) suggests PPHN or coarctation
Echo must confirm shunt physiology and exclude structural heart disease before iNO
iNO 20 ppm is the standard starting dose
ECMO criteria: OI greater than 25 to 40 despite iNO and HFOV, plus reversible cause
explanation.pearls: Always exclude infradiaphragmatic obstructed total anomalous pulmonary venous return on echo before attributing cyanosis to PPHN.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn

stem: A female infant born at 39+3 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth in a rural hospital is now 18 hours old. She had APGARs of 9 and 9 and was initially well, but the midwives have noted increasing central cyanosis over the last 2 hours, particularly with feeds. She remains alert with mild tachypnoea but no respiratory distress and no grunting. Preductal SpO2 is 78 percent and postductal SpO2 is 76 percent, both on room air. She does not improve on 8 L/min of facemask oxygen, with preductal SpO2 rising only to 84 percent after 10 minutes. Examination reveals a soft 2/6 ejection systolic murmur at the left upper sternal edge, palpable femoral pulses, no hepatomegaly, and a normal newborn examination otherwise. A chest radiograph shows clear lung fields and a normal cardiac silhouette. ECG shows sinus rhythm. The on-call neonatal retrieval consultant accepts the case for transfer.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 150 bpm, RR 60 /min, BP 64/40 mmHg, preductal SpO2 78 percent room air, 84 percent on 8 L/min O2
data_table.Examination: Centrally cyanosed, alert, mild tachypnoea, 2/6 ESM at LUSE, femoral pulses palpable equally, no hepatomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Hyperoxia test: preductal SpO2 84 percent and PaO2 78 mmHg after 10 minutes of 100 percent oxygen; CXR clear; ECG sinus rhythm

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management while awaiting retrieval and echocardiography?

options.text: Commence intravenous prostaglandin E1 infusion and prepare for intubation given the risk of apnoea
options.rationale: A cyanotic newborn who fails the hyperoxia test (PaO2 staying below 150 mmHg or 20 kPa) is presumed to have a duct-dependent cyanotic congenital heart lesion until echo proves otherwise; PGE1 0.05 microg/kg/min maintains ductal patency, with awareness of apnoea, hypotension and fever, per RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease

options.text: Continue facemask oxygen at 100 percent and observe for a further 4 hours before any intervention
options.rationale: Failed hyperoxia test indicates fixed right-to-left shunt; continued oxygen alone is inadequate and risks collapse if duct closes. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic CHD).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease

options.text: Commence inhaled nitric oxide at 20 ppm for presumed persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn
options.rationale: iNO is for PPHN with right-to-left ductal shunting on echo and structurally normal heart; presumptive PPHN before echo may worsen ductal-dependent CHD. Would be correct after echo confirms PPHN (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn

options.text: Give a 20 mL/kg normal saline bolus and arrange transthoracic echo in working hours next morning
options.rationale: Fluid bolus does not address the underlying shunt physiology; delaying echo risks ductal closure. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease

options.text: Administer indomethacin IV to close any patent ductus and reduce shunting
options.rationale: Closing duct in duct-dependent lesion is catastrophic; indomethacin is for haemodynamically significant PDA in preterms without duct-dependent CHD. Would be correct only in haemodynamically significant PDA without ductal-dependency (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Duct-dependent cyanotic congenital heart disease (transposition, pulmonary atresia, tricuspid atresia, severe Fallot, severe Ebstein) presents in the first hours to days with persistent cyanosis poorly responsive to oxygen. The hyperoxia test (PaO2 less than 150 mmHg / 20 kPa after 100 percent oxygen) supports cardiac cause. PGE1 0.05 microg/kg/min maintains the duct; lower doses (0.005 to 0.01 microg/kg/min) maintain an already open duct.
key_points: Hyperoxia test: PaO2 less than 150 mmHg suggests cardiac shunt
PGE1 side effects: apnoea (40 percent), fever, hypotension, jitteriness
Have intubation kit at bedside; transport by retrieval team
Always confirm with urgent echo before complex decisions
explanation.pearls: Differential cyanosis (preductal greater than postductal) suggests coarctation or interrupted arch; reverse differential cyanosis suggests TGA with coarctation.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease
RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn

stem: A term male infant born at 39+6 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth to a 27-year-old G1P0 woman is now 36 hours old on the postnatal ward of a tertiary maternity hospital. APGARs were 9 and 9 and the perinatal course was uncomplicated with no maternal infection, no chorioamnionitis, no prolonged ROM, no instrumental delivery, no resuscitation and a normal cord gas (arterial pH 7.32, base deficit 4 mmol/L). Birthweight was 3380 g. He has been breastfeeding well with audible swallows, with normal heelprick BSLs of 3.0 to 4.5 mmol/L throughout, and has had three wet nappies and meconium passed at 8 hours. At 36 hours he develops sudden onset rhythmic jerking of the right arm and right leg lasting around 90 seconds, with lip smacking and a brief apnoea with desaturation to 88 percent, followed by a similar focal motor seizure 20 minutes later. He is now post-ictal with reduced tone in the right limbs but rouseable to handling. He has not been cooled and has no risk factors for HIE. Sodium is 138 mmol/L, ionised calcium 1.25 mmol/L, magnesium 0.8 mmol/L, BSL 4.2 mmol/L, ammonia 60 micromol/L, blood gas pH 7.36 with normal lactate, and a sepsis screen including blood culture, FBC, CRP and lumbar puncture is in progress. EEG and cranial ultrasound are being arranged.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, HR 158 bpm, RR 50 /min, SpO2 98 percent on room air, BP 70/42 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Post-ictal, reduced tone right side, rouseable, no dysmorphism, anterior fontanelle soft
data_table.Investigations: Na 138 mmol/L, ionised Ca 1.25 mmol/L, Mg 0.8 mmol/L, BSL 4.2 mmol/L, ammonia 60 micromol/L, pH 7.36, lactate 1.6 mmol/L, FBC and CRP normal pending culture

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer rectal diazepam and observe for cessation of seizure activity
options.rationale: Diazepam is not first-line in neonatal seizures and rectal route is unreliable; phenobarbitone IV gives sustained levels and proven efficacy. Would never be correct in neonates (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Seizures).

options.text: Give intravenous calcium gluconate 10% empirically for presumed hypocalcaemic seizure
options.rationale: Ionised calcium 1.25 is within normal range; empirical calcium is not indicated and can cause arrhythmia if extravasated. Would be correct only with documented hypocalcaemia (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence pyridoxine 100 mg IV and withhold antiepileptic therapy until response is assessed
options.rationale: Pyridoxine is for refractory neonatal seizures unresponsive to standard antiepileptics; should not delay phenobarbitone. Would be correct as second-line after phenobarbitone failure (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: IV phenobarbitone load, blood/urine for metabolic and infection screen, LP, urgent MRI
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IV is first-line for confirmed neonatal seizures in Australian protocols; concurrently the cause must be sought (sepsis, metabolic, intracranial bleed or infarct, structural). MRI is preferred for parenchymal injury and stroke detection, per RCH CPG: Neonatal seizures and Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology.

options.text: Initiate therapeutic hypothermia at 33.5 deg C for 72 hours as initial seizure therapy
options.rationale: This baby does not meet cooling criteria (no perinatal hypoxia-ischaemia, normal cord gas, normal APGARs). Would be correct only with HIE features (Source: ANZNN).

explanation.summary: Causes of neonatal seizures, in rough order of frequency: HIE, ischaemic stroke and intracranial haemorrhage, infection (meningitis, encephalitis, sepsis), metabolic (hypoglycaemia, hypocalcaemia, hyponatraemia, inborn errors), structural, and pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy. A term infant without perinatal hypoxia and with normal initial bloods should prompt urgent MRI and LP. Phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IV is the first-line antiepileptic in Australia.
key_points: Subtle seizures (eye deviation, cycling, lip smacking, apnoea) are common in neonates
First-line: phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IV load, repeat 10 mg/kg up to total 40 mg/kg
Second-line: levetiracetam or phenytoin; pyridoxine for refractory
Always seek the cause: imaging, LP, metabolic screen, glucose, electrolytes, ammonia
explanation.pearls: Perinatal arterial ischaemic stroke commonly presents as focal seizures in the first 72 hours of life in a previously well term baby; MRI with DWI is diagnostic.

stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 woman is reviewed in the antenatal clinic at 36 weeks gestation by the midwifery and obstetric team. She and her partner have a written birth plan declining intramuscular vitamin K for their baby because of concerns about historical claims linking IM vitamin K with childhood leukaemia. They are willing to consider oral vitamin K as a compromise but want to know the specific regimen and the residual risk. The pregnancy has been uncomplicated with reassuring serial growth and screening, the mother is on no medications (no anticonvulsants, antibiotics, rifampicin, or anticoagulants), and there are no risk factors for fetal coagulopathy or maternal liver disease. Their first child received IM vitamin K and had no problems. They intend to exclusively breastfeed and the booking is for a planned vaginal birth at 39 weeks. They specifically ask you to compare the oral and IM regimens by Australian guidance and have read that the leukaemia link has been refuted.

data_table.Maternal history: G2P1, no anticoagulants, no antiepileptics, no rifampicin, no significant medical history
data_table.Plan: Booked for vaginal birth at 39 weeks if uncomplicated

lead_in: Which counselling and recommendation is most appropriate?

options.text: IM vitamin K 1 mg single dose at birth first-line; if declined, oral 1 mg at birth, day 3-5, and 4 weeks with residual-risk counselling
options.rationale: NHMRC and RCH guidance recommends single-dose IM vitamin K 1 mg at birth as the most reliable prophylaxis against classical and late vitamin K deficiency bleeding (VKDB). When IM is declined, the recommended oral alternative is THREE 1 mg oral doses (birth, day 3 to 5, and 4 weeks) - not a single oral dose - with documented informed consent including the higher residual late VKDB risk, particularly with exclusive breastfeeding and undetected cholestasis, per RCH CPG: Vitamin K prophylaxis and NHMRC Statement on Vitamin K.

options.text: Recommend a single oral 1 mg vitamin K dose given at birth as an equivalent alternative to IM with comparable late-VKDB protection
options.rationale: A single oral dose does not provide adequate protection against late VKDB; the recommended oral regimen requires three 1 mg doses (birth, day 3 to 5, 4 weeks), per RCH CPG: Vitamin K prophylaxis.

options.text: Recommend no vitamin K prophylaxis at all, since the historical leukaemia link is refuted and modern Australian background bleeding risk is negligible
options.rationale: Vitamin K is required to prevent classical and late VKDB; the refutation of the leukaemia link does not eliminate the need for prophylaxis, and declining is not equivalent to safe practice, per RCH CPG: Vitamin K prophylaxis and NHMRC Statement on Vitamin K.

options.text: Recommend maternal oral vitamin K 20 mg daily from 36 weeks until delivery as a substitute for any neonatal prophylaxis given at birth
options.rationale: Maternal supplementation does not provide adequate neonatal vitamin K stores or protect against late VKDB and is not a recommended substitute, per NHMRC Statement on Vitamin K.

options.text: Defer the decision until after delivery so the family can meet the baby and decide with the postnatal midwife in the hours after birth
options.rationale: Antenatal informed consent allows the family to consider evidence and ask questions in a low-pressure setting; deferring this discussion to the immediate postnatal period is not best practice and risks unprepared decision-making, per RCH CPG: Vitamin K prophylaxis.

explanation.summary: Haemorrhagic disease of the newborn (now vitamin K deficiency bleeding, VKDB) is preventable with prophylaxis. Classical VKDB occurs in days 1 to 7; late VKDB occurs from 2 weeks to 6 months and presents with intracranial haemorrhage in exclusively breastfed infants. IM vitamin K 1 mg as a single dose at birth is the gold standard; the oral alternative is 1 mg at birth, day 3 to 5, and 4 weeks. The historical leukaemia link from the 1990s has been refuted by multiple subsequent studies.
key_points: IM 1 mg single dose at birth is first-line
Oral regimen: 1 mg at birth, day 3 to 5, and 4 weeks; higher residual late VKDB risk
Late VKDB risk particularly in cholestasis (biliary atresia) and exclusive breastfeeding
Document consent process when families decline
explanation.pearls: If a baby on oral vitamin K develops cholestatic jaundice, switch to IM immediately.

stem: A male infant is born at 32+0 weeks by spontaneous vaginal birth to a 28-year-old G1P0 woman with preterm prelabour rupture of membranes 5 days before delivery. She was admitted for monitoring, received prophylactic erythromycin and a course of antenatal corticosteroids; specifically, two doses of antenatal betamethasone with the second dose 48 hours before delivery, and a single dose of magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection commenced 4 hours before delivery. There was no maternal fever, the CRP remained normal, and the white cell count was 11 x10^9/L pre-delivery. APGARs were 7 and 8 with brief CPAP via T-piece in the delivery room. Birthweight is 1640 g (50th centile for gestation). He is transferred to the level 3 NICU at 10 minutes of age. From birth he has had moderate work of breathing with intercostal recession, mild grunting, and SpO2 of 89 percent in room air. He is now on nasal CPAP 7 cm H2O with FiO2 0.35 delivered via short binasal prongs, has mild grunting that is gradually improving, and is haemodynamically stable with a mean BP of 38 mmHg. The team prepares to administer surfactant in the next 30 minutes.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 160 bpm, RR 65 /min on CPAP, SpO2 92 percent on FiO2 0.35, BP mean 38 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Mild grunting and intercostal recession, equal air entry, capillary refill 2 seconds, no peripheral cyanosis
data_table.Antenatal: Single course antenatal betamethasone 48 hours pre-delivery, MgSO4 for neuroprotection, no maternal fever, no chorioamnionitis

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate surfactant administration strategy in this infant?

options.text: Continue CPAP without surfactant unless FiO2 rises above 0.6 or work of breathing worsens
options.rationale: AU threshold for surfactant in preterm RDS is sustained FiO2 over 0.3 (under 28 weeks) or 0.3-0.4 (28-32 weeks); ignoring 0.35 in 32-weeker risks worsening RDS. Would be correct only with FiO2 under threshold (Source: RCH CPG: Surfactant).

options.text: Intubate, give surfactant, and continue mechanical ventilation for at least the first 24 hours
options.rationale: Prolonged ventilation after surfactant increases BPD risk in spontaneously breathing preterm; less invasive strategy preferred. Would be correct only in apnoea or failed CPAP (Source: ANZNN).

options.text: Repeat a second course of antenatal corticosteroid postnatally as IM betamethasone to the infant to enhance lung maturation
options.rationale: Postnatal corticosteroids in this context are inappropriate; antenatal steroids must be given to mother pre-delivery, and postnatal dexamethasone is for ventilator-dependent infants at risk of BPD. Would never be correct here (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Give nebulised surfactant via the CPAP circuit over 30 minutes
options.rationale: Nebulised surfactant remains investigational and not recommended outside trials in AU; tracheal delivery is required for proven efficacy. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Continue CPAP and give surfactant via thin catheter using LISA technique with spontaneous breathing
options.rationale: In a preterm infant breathing spontaneously on CPAP, LISA (also called MIST in some AU centres) allows surfactant administration via a thin tracheal catheter while CPAP and spontaneous breathing continue, and reduces the rate of mechanical ventilation and BPD compared with INSURE, per RCH CPG: Surfactant administration and Cochrane: LISA versus INSURE.

explanation.summary: Preterm RDS is managed primarily with antenatal corticosteroids, early CPAP from delivery room, and surfactant delivered to those needing it. LISA delivers surfactant via a thin catheter while the infant continues spontaneous breathing on CPAP, reducing mechanical ventilation and bronchopulmonary dysplasia compared with INSURE (intubate, surfactant, extubate). Threshold for surfactant is typically FiO2 sustained above 0.3 (less than 28 weeks) or 0.3 to 0.4 (28 to 32 weeks).
key_points: Antenatal betamethasone halves RDS, IVH and neonatal mortality
Early CPAP at 6 to 8 cm H2O is first-line respiratory support
LISA / MIST reduces BPD versus INSURE in spontaneously breathing infants
Surfactant dose: poractant alfa 200 mg/kg initial; beractant 100 mg/kg
explanation.pearls: A second course of antenatal corticosteroids is considered if more than 7 to 14 days have elapsed and delivery remains likely within 7 days at gestation under 32 weeks.
